text: Hi and welcome to This is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Roche from Contino.
Hey Fabio. So Roche to start can you give us more details around what Contino is doing?
Sure so Contino are a global digital transformation consultancy where we
specialize in helping highly regulated industries become better at people processes and technology.
Okay so we have a specific project here. Can you give us more details around what issue does it solve?
Sure so let's start off in the bottom left here with Control Tower. Now Control Tower is an AWS solution that helps you set up a landing zone.

Effectively helping you to define what a good AWS estate looks like and that's really important so you can cover things around security, logging, auditing and also gives you the ability to scale out your workloads as the demand in your business
increases. Okay what problem are you solving with Flight Controller? Yeah good question so Flight Controller aims to visualize the business impact that your landing zone is having.
Think of it as a way to have a health check on the performance of your landing zone. Cool so how do you deploy it? How does it work? Can you give us more technical details around it?
Yeah sure so Flight Controller is an extension to Control Tower and takes 90 minutes to deploy into your environment by setting up a separate Flight Controller management account.
Oh that's good so all this stuff gets deployed and starts working after 90 minutes.
Correct. That's good that's very good. Okay and now that works how do you ingest the metrics? How do you fetch them?
Yeah so let's take the scenario of a SRE team needing to deploy and bend a new account. Now this account could be needed for testing purposes or deploying new workloads. In this case here,
we can use AWS subscriptions to CloudWatch events and we can listen for an event as soon as a new code pipeline is kicked off.
We'll take that raw data and we'll put that into EventBridge. Okay.

From there we will then use a Lambda to transform that raw data so we can pluck out key information such as the resource that's been triggered such as in this case CodePipeline, the time in which that happened and other key metadata.

Okay so the data is now enriched. Yes. I assume now you want to store it somewhere. Yes. Okay where do you have no storing it? Yeah it's a good question. So we actually store the data in two places. The first of which is into DynamoDB.
And this is just so we can have the raw data so that in the future if we need to access this data in any way we have it available in one central location.

Okay that's historical data. Yes. I assume you have something for visualization as well. Absolutely. So if we go back to a control tower and our sprawling landing zone and our business you can imagine that there's thousands of events that are happening all the time.

And that's where we take advantage of time stream. And time stream is a really good way to measure time series data. And so in this case here when a CodePipeline kicks off we can sort that into time stream and also when that CodePipeline is finished again we can aggregate that data in one central location.
Okay so a specialized database to store something that gets ingested very fast. Yes. Yes. And then you will plug visualization into it right? Exactly that. So this is where we use Grafana.
And utilizing its SQL like syntax so we can produce dashboards and visualizations. In this case again so we can see how performance our SRE and DevOps team are being in the account vending process.
Okay. So now we have the visualization and I have everything. How fast is this to ingest the data?
Yeah. So it's good to mention here Fabio that this whole architecture is serverless. Oh nice.
So from the point of an event being listened to in CodePipeline or systems manager to it being visualized on Grafana takes under 20 seconds.
And you are just paying for the events that you want to ingest. Exactly.

So yeah you are in control but you are paying the right price. Yeah. Okay that makes sense. Is there anything, any other number that you could share about the infrastructure or any special area where you work tirelessly to maintain it or to make it better?
Yeah. So out of the box flight controller supports key KPIs and metrics such as account vending times or patching times.
Okay. But the beauty of this architecture that it's fully extensible by the client. And by that we mean that you can define your own metrics by subscribing to the right events in your AWS account.
And then shipping them into EventBridge and then defining further landers to transform that data in that specific case.
So really depending on the needs of your SRE team, you can have the right metrics to value what good looks like.
So the more the metrics, the more the lambda that will be executed. You just execute the one that you want and you just pay for the metrics that really matter to you.
Exactly that. I see that it's pretty extensible. Would this part of the architecture work even for other metrics? Something that is not related to control tower that maybe I want to measure?
Yeah, it's a really good point. So it supports control tower, but there's no reason that flight controller cannot support other types of landing zones and other third party SaaS integrations.
So you can have a great visibility not over just your AWS account, but your whole entire backend process.
Yeah, anything that sits there and it's meaningful to the special customer.
Yeah. No, that really makes sense. I think it's a very compelling architecture. I like the fact that it can be extended.
So it's totally customizable for the end customers.
Yeah. So thank you very much for sharing the architecture with us, Roche.
Thank you, Fabio.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
This is your architecture.
This is my architecture.
